title: Chelonanthera clypeata
intro: Chelonanthera clypeata, also known as Butterfly Pea or Asian Pigeonwings, is a flowering plant species that belongs to the Acanthaceae family. This plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine for its potential health benefits, including reducing inflammation and improving cognitive function, as well as exhibiting antioxidant and anti-anxiety properties. The bioactive constituents of Chelonanthera clypeata include flavonoids, alkaloids, and saponins, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with this plant include teas, capsules, and extracts, which are often used to promote relaxation, enhance memory, and support overall well-being.
taxonomy: Chelonanthera clypeata is a species of flowering plant in the family Acanthaceae. It belongs to the genus Chelonanthera, which consists of a few species of flowering plants. The family Acanthaceae is a large and diverse group of plants commonly known as acanthus or acanthus family. This family is further divided into several subfamilies, with Acanthoideae being the largest subfamily. The classification of Chelonanthera clypeata reflects its evolutionary relationships and adaptations to its environment.
uses: Chelonanthera clypeata is used in traditional medicine in various parts of the world, particularly in Asia, where it is believed to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The plant is said to be effective in treating a range of health issues, including fever, sore throats, and skin conditions. In some cultures, Chelonanthera clypeata is used to make medicinal teas, infusions, and poultices. The plant's roots and leaves are often used in traditional remedies, and it is sometimes combined with other herbs to enhance its medicinal properties. Further research is needed to fully understand the plant's potential health benefits and its effectiveness in treating various medical conditions.

side_effects: Chelonanthera clypeata, a plant native to tropical regions, may cause skin irritation and allergic reactions in some individuals due to its sap. Handling the plant can lead to skin redness, itching, and blistering in people with sensitive skin.
